title: Design Twitter
overview: Build Twitter's backend from scratch — posting tweets, following users, and generating home feeds. The architecture will evolve significantly as scale grows.

levels.title: Basic Twitter
levels.constraints: 10K DAU. Users post tweets and follow others. Home feed shows tweets from people they follow. No real-time requirement.
levels.feedbackHint: Solid foundation! At 1M+ users, querying the DB for every feed load will grind to a halt.
levels.aiContextHint: Level 1 is a simple monolithic design. A server stores tweets and follows in a relational DB. Home feed is a JOIN query. Accept any design with server + database.

levels.title: High Read Volume
levels.constraints: 10M DAU. Read:write ratio is 100:1. Home feed queries across millions of follows are timing out under load.
levels.feedbackHint: Caching pre-computed feeds per user massively cuts DB load. Next challenge: celebrities with millions of followers.
levels.aiContextHint: Level 2 adds a cache to store pre-computed home feeds per user. When someone posts, fan-out writes the tweet into each follower's cache feed. API Gateway handles auth and rate limiting.

levels.title: Fan-out Problem
levels.constraints: A celebrity with 10M followers posts a tweet. Your naive fan-out tries to write 10M cache entries synchronously — it times out. Users see the tweet hours late.
levels.feedbackHint: Async fan-out via a message queue (NoSQL as queue) decouples posting from delivery. Workers drain the queue gradually. Good thinking!
levels.aiContextHint: Level 3 uses an async fan-out pattern. The tweet is written to a queue (NoSQL). Workers asynchronously push to follower caches. This prevents write timeouts for high-follower accounts.

levels.title: Hybrid Fan-out
levels.constraints: Async fan-out is slow for celebrities — followers still wait minutes for new tweets. Regular users get instant delivery but celebrities are a second-class experience.
levels.feedbackHint: The hybrid approach — push for regular users, pull for celebrities — is how Twitter actually works. Adding CDN for media makes it production-grade. One more challenge remains.
levels.aiContextHint: Level 4 uses a hybrid fan-out: push (write to follower caches) for regular users, pull (merge celebrity tweets at read time) for celebrities. CDN serves media and static assets.

levels.title: Content Moderation
levels.constraints: Harmful and restricted content reaches followers before it's detected. Moderation must happen before fan-out, but can't block the post response. It must be async.
levels.feedbackHint: You've designed a production-grade Twitter! The Reverse Proxy intercepts content before fan-out, routing it to a moderation pipeline. Tweets enter a 'pending' state until cleared.
levels.aiContextHint: Level 5 adds a content moderation pipeline. A Reverse Proxy (or internal gateway) routes new tweets through a moderation service (ML + rules engine) before fan-out. Cleared tweets are then delivered normally.
